---
title: "Spatial Visualization & Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis"
subtitle: "DMgtAsia · Session-II"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
  revealjs:
    output-file: slides-session2-spatial-viz-esda.html
    theme: [default, dmgt.scss]
    logo: dmgt_asia_logo.png
    footer: "DMgtAsia · Spatial Epidemiology"
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    progress: true
    transition: fade
    scrollable: true
    code-line-numbers: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
    incremental: false
    embed-resources: false
engine: knitr
webr:
  show-startup-message: false
filters:
  - webr
other-links:
  - text: "View as slides"
    href: slides-session2-spatial-viz-esda.html
    icon: easel2
---

# Part 1 · Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles

## Why the map comes first

-   In spatial analysis, the map is the primary instrument.
-   A good map encodes a variable so the eye can read pattern, outliers, and gaps.
-   Before any statistic, we *look*.

::: aside
The map is to spatial analysis what the histogram is to ordinary EDA.
:::

---

## Cartographic Principles

GeoSWG best practices for public health cartography:

-   Visual contrast
-   Legibility
-   Figure-Ground Orientation
-   Hierarchical Organization
-   Balance

These keep map products high-quality and consistent across a team.

---

## Design Principles in Practice

![](images/designprinciples_2-lg.png)

---

## Good vs Poor Design

::: {layout-ncol="2"}
![](images/designprinciples_3.jpg)

![](images/designprinciples_4.jpg)

![](images/designprinciples_5.jpg)

![](images/designprinciples_6.jpg)
:::

---

## Cartographic Guidelines for Public Health

::: columns
::: {.column width="42%"}
-   Source: CDC, Atlanta
-   Map elements:
    -   Title and borders
    -   North arrow / graticule / scale
    -   Inset maps
    -   Labels and legend
-   Other elements:
    -   Data sources, dates, projection
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
![](images/map_elements_1.jpg){width="100%"}

<https://www.cdc.gov/dhdsp/maps/gisx/resources/cartographic_guidelines.pdf>
:::
:::

---

## The Choropleth

> A thematic map in which values of a variable are encoded with a colour gradient.

-   The spatial counterpart of the histogram.
-   Three choices decide whether it informs or misleads:
    -   **What** you map: rates, not raw counts
    -   **How** you classify: the breaks (quantile, equal-interval, Jenks)
    -   **Which** colours: sequential vs diverging, colour-blind safe

---

## Rates, not counts

-   Counts mostly track population size, so a count map is often just a population map.
-   Map **rates** (per 1000, per 100k) or proportions to compare places fairly.
-   Small-population areas give unstable rates: a single case swings them. Note this when you read the map.

---

## Colour, classification, accessibility

-   **Sequential** palette for low-to-high; **diverging** for departure from a midpoint.
-   Use colour-blind-safe palettes (`viridis`) and keep classes few (4 to 6).
-   The same data with different breaks tells different stories: always state the scheme.

---

## A map is spatial data

```{r}
#| label: districts-map
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "62%"
library(sf)
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

districts <- read_rds(here("spatial_files", "india_district_sf.rds"))

ggplot(districts) +
  geom_sf(linewidth = 0.05, fill = "grey95", colour = "grey40") +
  theme_void()
```

District polygons read with `sf`. Geometry plus attributes is all we need to start mapping.

---

## Building a choropleth (pattern to reuse)

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
districts |>                                  # an sf object
  left_join(indicator_df, by = "district") |> # attach the variable
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = stunting), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", direction = -1) +
  theme_void()
```

You build exactly this in **Exercise 6** (NFHS stunting). The same recipe drives the maps in
the rest of the module.

# Part 2 · Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)

## What is ESDA?

-   The spatial extension of Tukey's exploratory data analysis.
-   Describe and visualise spatial distributions, find atypical locations, and reveal patterns
    of spatial association and heterogeneity (Anselin, 1999).
-   It is **hypothesis-generating, not hypothesis-testing**.

::: aside
ESDA sits at the front of the workflow, before any spatial model.
:::

---

## Why spatial data needs its own EDA

Ordinary EDA assumes observations are independent. Spatial data usually are not.

-   **Spatial dependence:** near things are more related than distant things
    (Tobler's First Law, 1970).
-   **Spatial heterogeneity:** distributions and relationships change across space.

---

## Two cautions

-   **Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP):** results depend on how areas are bounded and
    aggregated.
-   **Ecological fallacy:** an area-level pattern need not hold for individuals within the
    area.

Name both when you present a map.

---

## Exploratory vs Confirmatory

-   **ESDA (now):** descriptive, visual, generates hypotheses about *where* and *how* values
    cluster or differ.
-   **Confirmatory (later):** spatial regression / econometrics tests those hypotheses
    (Anselin, 1988; Haining, 2003).

Keep the two stages separate.

---

## A short history

-   **Tukey (1977):** exploratory data analysis as visual, hypothesis-free description.
-   **Openshaw et al. (1987):** the Geographical Analysis Machine, automated cluster search.
-   **Interactive graphics:** REGARD, MANET, maps linked to plots (Bailey & Gatrell, 1995).
-   **Software:** SpaceStat, then **GeoDa** (Anselin et al., 2006), and **`spdep`** in R.

---

## Core ESDA: three ways of looking

1.  **Map** the spatial distribution (the choropleth from Part 1).
2.  **Link** plots to the map: brush a box plot or scatter plot and the same areas highlight.
3.  **Spot** atypical locations: a value unlike its neighbours is a spatial outlier.

These lead to one question: *is there spatial pattern at all?*

---

## Neighbours and the spatial lag

-   To make a method spatial, encode geography as a **spatial weights matrix** `W`:
    who is a neighbour, and how strongly.
-   The **spatial lag** of a variable is the (weighted) average of its neighbours.
-   Plotting a value against its spatial lag is the first quantitative ESDA step.

---

## Global autocorrelation, by hand

Change the values and re-run. Watch Moran's I move from independence (near 0) toward
clustering (toward +1).

```{webr-r}
# Four regions in a row: 1-2-3-4
y <- c(2, 3, 8, 9)                       # try c(2, 9, 3, 8) for a "checkerboard"

W <- matrix(c(0,1,0,0,
              1,0,1,0,
              0,1,0,1,
              0,0,1,0), nrow = 4, byrow = TRUE)   # who neighbours whom
Wr <- W / rowSums(W)                     # row-standardised weights

z   <- y - mean(y)                       # centre the values
lag <- as.vector(Wr %*% y)               # spatial lag = neighbour average
lag

moran_I <- sum(z * (Wr %*% z)) / sum(z^2)
moran_I                                  # global Moran's I
```

::: aside
Live in the browser via webR. No install, no server. The same cell embeds in Moodle as an iframe.
:::

---

## Reading global Moran's I

-   `I > 0`: similar values sit together (clustering of highs and of lows).
-   `I ≈ 0`: no spatial pattern (spatial randomness).
-   `I < 0`: neighbours are dissimilar (a checkerboard).

It answers *yes/no, is there pattern*. It does **not** say *where*.

---

## ESDA in public health

-   Map disease rates, flag candidate hot spots, and form hypotheses about drivers before
    modelling (Delmelle & Kanaroglou, 2013).
-   Central to surveillance: which areas may be at risk?
-   Extends to space and time (for example, dengue across districts of Punjab, India).
-   Connects directly to NFHS and notification data at district / facility level.

---

## What comes next (later session)

-   From one **global** number to **local** indicators that say *where* the clusters are:
    local Moran's I and the LISA map, and Getis-Ord hot spots (Anselin, 1995).
-   That is the Spatial Data Analysis & Clustering session. We stop here, at the exploratory
    stage.

---

## Key references {.smaller}

-   Tukey, J.W. (1977). *Exploratory Data Analysis*. Addison-Wesley.
-   Tobler, W. (1970). A computer movie simulating urban growth. *Economic Geography* 46.
-   Anselin, L. (1988). *Spatial Econometrics*. Kluwer.
-   Anselin, L. (1999). Interactive techniques and ESDA. In *Geographical Information Systems*.
-   Bailey, T.C. & Gatrell, A.C. (1995). *Interactive Spatial Data Analysis*. Longman.
-   Haining, R. (2003). *Spatial Data Analysis: Theory and Practice*. Cambridge.
-   Anselin, Syabri & Kho (2006). GeoDa. *Geographical Analysis* 38.

# Recap

## Recap

-   The map is the first instrument: design it with care (contrast, hierarchy, honest rates,
    safe colour).
-   ESDA spatialises EDA and is hypothesis-generating.
-   Spatial data is special: dependence and heterogeneity; mind MAUP and the ecological fallacy.
-   Look three ways (map, link, spot outliers), then ask global Moran's I: is there pattern?
-   *Where* the clusters are comes next, with LISA.
